name: ES Allianz
classification_notes: Described as inspired by early sans serifs that preceded the neo-grotesque style, with organic and subtly hand-drawn qualities; sits between pre-grotesque antique letterforms and modernist functional type.
summary: A grotesque sans with roots in pre-neo-grotesque antique letterforms, drawn by David Mamie with an organic, slightly hand-crafted quality.
description: Originally drafted in the 2010s by David Mamie with input from Nicola Todeschini, ES Allianz draws on early sans serif forms that preceded the polished neutrality of the neo-grotesque tradition. Seven weights from Extralight to Extrabold come with italics, and the character set includes a generous range of alternates such as single-storey 'a', a swing-legged 'R', alternate digits and various symbol sets. The result sits between functional modernism and a more undogmatic, expressive approach to sans serif letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Allianz as inspired by early sans serifs that preceded the neo-grotesque style, aiming to spotlight the originality and sharpness of that era's characters. They frame it as a typeface that could have belonged to the New Typography, developed on undogmatic principles with surrealistic moments, and positioned as a contemporary alternative to Antique classics.

name: ES Build
classification_notes: ES Build is explicitly geometric in construction, drawing directly from Herbert Bayer's Bauhaus Universal typeface, with circular bowls, reduced terminals and optical corrections typical of the geometric sans genre.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in Herbert Bayer's Bauhaus Universal typeface, offering 18 OpenType character sets to slide between strict geometric reduction and more readable neutrality.
description: Designed by Xavier Erni of Neo Neo with assistance from Arthur Schwarz, ES Build takes Bayer's terminal-free bowl construction as its starting point while adding uppercase letters and a 675-glyph palette that Bayer's original deliberately refused. The 18 character sets, selectable via OpenType features, let users move between a full Bauhaus treatment, a standard interpretation and a neutral reading, all within the same four-weight family. A variable font file is available on request with the complete family purchase.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Build as a geometric font inspired by Herbert Bayer's research into a universal typeface during his time as master of the Bauhaus typography workshop from 1925 to 1928. They note that Bayer stripped his design to bare essentials by removing uppercase letters and modifying the juncture between stems and bowls on letters such as b, d, p and q. ES Build retains that terminal feature but offers a less radical, more readable interpretation, with 675 glyphs, four weights with italics and a wide set of alternative characters via OpenType.

name: ES Dokument
classification_notes: Directly inspired by early 20th-century German industrial grotesques, with distinctive features such as high-waisted capitals and a hooked lowercase r placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition with a contemporary inflection.
summary: A 60-style grotesque superfamily drawn from early 20th-century German industrial sources, with high-waisted capitals, a distinctive hooked lowercase r and an outstroked single-storey a giving it clear personality across three widths.
description: Designed by Xavier Erni and Arthur Schwarz between 2020 and 2022, the family spans condensed, standard and extended widths with ten weights each and matching italics, covering everything from tight package-insert text to large-format headlines. OpenType stylistic sets allow angled terminal strokes on key capitals (E, F, L, T, Z) that pull the design closer to its industrial grotesque origins, alongside barred I and J and softer alternate a and r forms for a more neutral setting. A variable font file is available on request with purchase of the complete family.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Dokument as a superfamily of 60 styles that draws its curves from German industrial grotesques of the early 20th century while offering a contemporary and original interpretation. The high-waisted capitals, outstroked lowercase a and hooked lowercase r give the face strong character, and OpenType features allow users to toggle between the default cut and alternates including angled-terminal capitals and softer lowercase forms. Three graduating widths — condensed, standard and extended — each carry ten weights with italics, making it suitable for miniature texts on package inserts through to extrabold billboard headlines.

name: ES Face
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly roots Face in 18th-century transitional serif heritage, with drop terminals as the default and a secondary 'straight' set offering sharper, more contemporary finishes.
summary: A transitional serif by Alex Dujet with drop terminals as default and an alternative straight set aimed at text composition and titling.
description: Developed from 2016 with commercial word processing in mind, the design draws on 18th-century transitional models while incorporating partially geometric drawing at the terminals. Four OpenType stylistic sets allow switching between drop terminals, straight cuts, an alternate 't' and a classic ampersand, giving the family a wide tonal range across its ten weights. The italic adds a distinct cutting angle through the stems, marking it out from more neutral text serifs.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Face as a serif balancing transitional heritage with contemporary finishes, developed by Alex Dujet initially as a tool for reading comfort in commercial word processing. The family offers a default drop-terminal set and a 'straight' set for a sharper look, with an assertive italic angle designed to highlight words within longer compositions. Twenty styles across four OpenType sets cover an extensive range of Latin languages including Vietnamese.

name: ES Klarheit Grotesk
classification_notes: Draws explicitly on Bauhaus geometric principles and 1960s Swiss modernism, placing it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition with extensive OpenType alternates that allow it to shift between neutral and more assertive registers.
summary: A geometric sans by Alex Dujet that blends Bauhaus geometry with 1960s Swiss modernism across 8 weights, with 13 OpenType stylistic sets for shifting between neutral and expressive character.
description: Developed over five years by Alex Dujet, the family sits between two modernist traditions: the geometric forms of the Bauhaus and the rational precision of Swiss typography from the 1960s. Thirteen stylistic sets offer alternates including a schoolbook 'a', angular 'G', sweeping 'y' and rounded punctuation, letting designers tune the typeface between clean neutrality and more distinctive graphic positioning. The 16-style family covers broad Latin Extended coverage including Vietnamese and Chinese Pinyin, with full italic cuts across all eight weights.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Klarheit Grotesk as oscillating between two major standards of modernism: Bauhaus geometric solutions and 1960s Swiss typographic conventions. After five years of development, designer Alex Dujet defined several historical frameworks around the basic design, resulting in 13 versatile stylistic sets whose combinations allow progressive design choices ranging from neutral to more assertive graphic positioning.

name: ES Klarheit Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced grotesque with geometric and Swiss modernist influences, explicitly designed as a monocase companion to the proportional ES Klarheit Grotesk family, drawing from Bauhaus geometry and 1960s Swiss typographic standards.
summary: A monospaced companion to ES Klarheit Grotesk, designed by Alex Dujet with geometric Bauhaus influences and 1960s Swiss modernist proportions, featuring a monocase structure and 12 stylistic sets.
description: Built as an extension of the Klarheit project, this mono variant applies a monocase treatment to the same geometric formula underpinning the proportional family. Twelve stylistic sets offer a range of letterform finishes, including alternate G cuts, sweeping y, schoolbook forms and rounded punctuation, allowing shifts between neutral and more assertive settings. The fixed-width construction makes it well suited to vertical alignment requirements in UI, coding or tabular contexts.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Klarheit Grotesk Mono as an inevitable extension of the Klarheit project, oscillating between the two typographic standards established in the proportional family. The Klarheit project seeks to meet the requirements of a contemporary modernism, revisiting geometrical solutions from the Bauhaus and key standards from 1960s Swiss modernism. Designer Alex Dujet applied a monocase version to the formula, with 12 versatile stylistic sets that match the proportional version and enable progressive design choices between neutral and assertive graphic positioning.

name: ES Klarheit Plakat
classification_notes: A super-heavy display companion to the Klarheit Grotesk and Kurrent families, covering only the Extrabold-to-Poster weight range; its sole purpose is titling and short-text composition at large sizes, placing it firmly in the display category despite its grotesque structural roots.
summary: A four-weight display companion to the Klarheit system, designed exclusively for heavy titling work with 20 stylistic sets drawn from the Grotesk and Kurrent subfamilies.
description: Spanning Extrabold, Black, Extrablack and Poster, ES Klarheit Plakat sits at the heavy end of Alex Dujet's Klarheit project and is built around short-text composition and poster work. Its 20 OpenType sets pull alternates from both the Grotesk and Kurrent branches of the family, ranging from schoolbook forms and rounded punctuation to incised strokes and ligature options, giving designers considerable control over the typeface's character. Wide Latin Extended coverage includes Vietnamese and Chinese Pinyin, making it practical across Western and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Klarheit Plakat as emerging from the multiple possibilities of the flagship Klarheit Grotesk project and its Kurrent sub-family. Alex Dujet designed it as a playful tool for bold titlings and small amounts of text, synthesising the particular finishes of both Klarheit versions alongside more radical glyph decisions. Eleven stylistic sets come from the base Grotesk and Kurrent versions, extended by nine further sets that add or remove expression, progressing from an assertive Kurrent default through schoolbook, rounded and squared forms, ligatures, ampersand variants and a Grotesk-oriented conclusion.

name: ES Klarheit Grotesk Paneuro
classification_notes: The typeface blends Bauhaus geometric construction with 1960s Swiss modernist conventions, placing it squarely in the geometric grotesque tradition with a distinctly pan-European character.
summary: A geometric grotesque by Alex Dujet that draws on Bauhaus construction and 1960s Swiss modernism, with 13 OpenType stylistic sets offering movement between neutral and assertive letter forms across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Developed over five years, the typeface positions itself between two historical models: the geometric rigour of the Bauhaus and the typographic conventions of Swiss modernism in the 1960s. Thirteen stylistic sets cover alternates for forms including an angular-cut G, schoolbook characters, kurrent K and sweeping y, allowing the same weight to read as restrained or expressive depending on the combination selected. Language coverage is extensive, spanning Vietnamese and Pinyin alongside Cyrillic scripts for Belarusian, Ukrainian, Kazakh and Mongolian among others, and full Greek.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Klarheit Grotesk Paneuro as oscillating between two major standards, revisiting geometrical solutions from the Bauhaus and key typographic standards from 1960s Swiss modernism. Alex Dujet refined the design over five years, arriving at 16 styles and 13 stylistic sets that combine to offer progressive design choices between neutral and more assertive graphic positioning.

name: ES Nein
classification_notes: An extra-bold, highly condensed single-weight display face drawing directly from the heritage of nineteenth-century wood types, with diagonal features and ink trap variants characteristic of that tradition.
summary: A single-weight extra-bold condensed display face by Alex Dujet, reinterpreting the visual language of nineteenth-century wood types with two stylistic sets.
description: Drawn from wood type heritage, the design offers stark diagonal features in its default set and a softer ink-trap variant that reduces contrast across its very black forms. It has been used in commissions for the New York Times Magazine and projects within alternative culture. Coverage extends across Latin Extended-A, supporting a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Nein as a reinterpretation of wood type heritage, developed by Alex Dujet over several years. The default stylistic set references classic wood type through pronounced diagonal details, while the ink-trap set offers a lighter contrast against the typeface's very black base. Two stylistic sets add variety, and the Latin Extended-A coverage was a deliberate choice to allow assertive typesetting across a wide range of languages.

name: ES Park
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies it as a Slab Serif (Mechanistic/Antique/Egyptian), with thick block-like serifs drawn slightly narrower than typical slabs and curves referencing Clarendon and American Typewriter.
summary: A slab serif in eight weights drawing on Clarendon and American Typewriter, with mini-serif terminals and a round-terminal OpenType alternate.
description: Designed by Xavier Erni of Neo Neo and developed by Lisa Schultz, ES Park sits between baroque warmth and mechanical precision through curves referencing Clarendon and American Typewriter. Its serifs are slightly narrower than typical slabs, and contrast increases with weight, keeping lighter styles readable at small sizes while heavier cuts carry real impact. An OpenType stylistic set swaps the default mini-serif terminals for softer, rounder forms, and a variable font is available to purchasers of the complete family.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Park as a 'more or less' baroque serif with thick, block-like serifs inspired by Clarendon and American Typewriter, classifiable as a Slab Serif or Egyptian. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Extra Bold with matching italics and four OpenType sets, including round terminals for a softer look. They position it as suitable for both distinctive branding and literary typesetting, with strong screen and print legibility across its range.

name: ES Peak
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist typeface with a linear construction, blending geometric and calligraphic curves; sharp, pointed terminals distinguish it from conventional grotesque or geometric models.
summary: A Swiss humanist sans by Xavier Erni that fuses geometric construction with calligraphic curves and sharp, pointed terminals across eight weights.
description: Initiated in 2013, the family spans Thin to Extrabold with matching italics, and a broad set of alternative glyphs allows designers to shift the aesthetic towards either geometry or calligraphy. A companion subfamily, Peak Rounded, trades the sharp details for softer, more organic forms. The typeface is intended to perform at text sizes on screen and in print while retaining a distinct character through its pointed detailing.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Peak as a linear typeface with sharp, pointed details that merges geometric and calligraphic curves. Initiated in 2013 by Xavier Erni of Neo Neo studio, it was developed with the aim of offering a contemporary take on humanist fonts. A wide range of alternative glyphs lets designers tune the aesthetic towards geometry or calligraphy, and the family is complemented by Peak Rounded, which introduces rounder, softer and more organic details.

name: ES Quarz
classification_notes: A Didone display face with hybrid influences drawn from American 1970s design, prioritising headline use through chain ligatures and swash forms rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight Didone display face by Alex Dujet that grafts American 1970s design quirks onto classical high-contrast serif structure through a system of chained ligatures and swash forms.
description: Designed by Alex Dujet, ES Quarz sits somewhere between Didot and 1970s American lettering, with five OpenType stylistic sets, contextual and discretionary ligatures, and automatic swash positional forms intended for titling work. The chain ligature system links successive letter combinations, producing runs of connected glyphs that give headlines an idiosyncratic, almost calligraphic rhythm. Available in a single Regular weight, it is a narrow-use face suited to luxury branding and editorial display contexts.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Quarz as a spontaneous, instinctive project in the Didone field, designed by Alex Dujet and positioned as a hybrid rather than a classic Didot. The goal was to create something distinctive for headlines, referencing American 1970s design alongside Didone conventions. Five stylistic sets, swash positional forms, and a chain ligature system allow letters to link together in varied combinations.

name: ES Rebond Grotesque
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif drawing on both Germanic grotesque tradition and French-Swiss typographic sensibility, with calligraphic influences visible in the ductus of certain glyphs.
summary: A French-Swiss grotesque by Roger Gaillard that balances Germanic neutrality with calligraphic warmth across nine weights and twelve OpenType stylistic sets.
description: Designed over five years by Roger Gaillard, it sits within the grotesque tradition whilst introducing a distinctly French-Swiss character through its curves and stroke connections. Twelve stylistic sets offer alternate forms for characters including 'a', 'g', 'k', 'f', 'Q' and '&', allowing designers to shift the typographic register from classical to more expressive. The extended Latin character set covers Vietnamese and Pinyin alongside broad European language support.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe Rebond Grotesque as simultaneously strict and flexible, with an assertive movement expressed through curves and links that brings a French-Swiss impetus to the Germanic grotesque. After five years in development, Roger Gaillard's typeface addresses calligraphic design principles by incrementally transforming the ductus of the Latin alphabet, offering a balanced contemporary text face with a palette of alternate glyphs for solving varied design problems.

name: ES Replan Variform
classification_notes: A variable font that mutates across three masters through eight weight stops, with alternate character sets drawing on fine arts and design culture references; its eclectic, idiosyncratic structure places it firmly in the display/experimental category rather than any conventional sans or serif tradition.
summary: A variable font by Alex Dujet that shifts across three typographic masters and eight weight stops, with six OpenType alternate sets referencing fine arts and design culture.
description: Designed by Alex Dujet for Extraset, it organises three interpolation masters into a continuous variable axis spanning Slanted through Extrablack, with each stop offering a distinct typographic atmosphere in a single file. Six OpenType feature sets including alternate uppercase and lowercase forms and a special 'Replanned' set allow further graphic variation beyond weight. The typeface is explicitly positioned as a graphic tool rather than a text workhorse, paying tribute to iconic forms drawn from art and design history.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Replan Variform as a variable font project that evolves between several typographic structures, designed to explore multiple atmospheres within a single file. Alex Dujet began with an idiosyncratic variable concept before developing a more eclectic and functional framework organised around three masters. Six OpenType feature sets offer selectable alternate characters and a special 'Replanned' set, making it a tool suited to diverse graphic contexts.

name: ES Schwarzweiss
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned within the Swiss Grotesk tradition, drawing on classical references and formal reduction consistent with the neo-grotesque genre, with attention to positive and negative space balance in the manner of Frutiger.
summary: A Swiss Grotesk designed by Alex Dujet across ten weights and matching italics, guided by Frutiger's theory of positive and negative space balance.
description: Built on classical Swiss Grotesk references, the family offers ten weights from Skeleton to Extrabold with corresponding italics and a variable font, totalling 20 styles. Ten stylistic sets give access to alternate letterforms and allow shifts between neutral text setting and more graphic titling. The drawing is rationalised to suppress visual noise while preserving a distinct minimalist character across broad Latin coverage.
foundry_blurb: Extraset describe ES Schwarzweiss as an exercise in formal reduction that puts the Swiss Grotesk genre in tension with contemporary requirements. Conceived by Alex Dujet and refined across multiple personal and commissioned projects, the family offers broad language coverage, a variable font, and ten stylistic sets that modulate readability or allow for a more graphic approach to titles. The name references Adrian Frutiger's black-and-white theory of positive and negative space in letterform design.
